aiTitle: Fix MCP validator blocking deep log greps

content.text:
The root cause is permission prompt fatigue — after ~10–15 unapproved tool calls, the harness gets increasingly conservative and starts blocking calls it would have allowed earlier. The fix is to pre-approve the tools that get blocked so they never hit the interactive validator.

Two options:

**1. Targeted fix (recommended)** — use the `update-config` skill to add the specific MCP tools to the allowlist. Tell it something like: "allow the synology-monitor MCP tools and bash grep/log commands without prompting."

**2. Automated scan** — run `/fewer-permission-prompts`, which reads your recent transcripts, finds the most common tool calls that triggered permission prompts, and adds them to `.claude/settings.json` automatically.

The second is lower-effort and catches things you might miss. Want me to run it now?

content.text:
No allowlist exists at all right now — so **every** tool call is hitting the interactive permission validator. That's the actual mechanism behind what your sessions are calling "degradation." There's no real "session degrades after 10–15 calls" effect in the harness; what happens is unapproved calls accumulate and the validator blocks them, and the "~10–15-call limit" got written into a runbook as folklore. So there are two things to fix: the permissions (the real cause) and the runbook (the misdiagnosis that's making sessions give up).

Let me find that runbook and confirm what tools actually get blocked.

content.content:
160	Why: Coolify restarts wipe in-memory sessions; statelessness eliminates "stale
161	session 404" after redeploys, and a stateless transport for `GET /mcp` without a
162	session ID stopped the claude.ai proxy's 4-minute hang (see incidents).
163	Do not change because: stateful mode brings back session-resume bugs and forces
164	dynamic tool registration that Claude clients ignore.
165	
166	### NAS MCP exposes 5 tools but has a 119-definition registry
167	Looks like: most tools are broken/unregistered.
168	Actually: deliberate lazy-load. `tools/list` returns only `tool_search`,
169	`invoke_tool`, `run_command`, `check_disk_space`, `restart_nas_api`. Clients
170	discover via `tool_search`, execute via `invoke_tool({name,target,args})`.
171	Why: pre-loading 119 schemas put ~50k tokens into every session and degraded it
172	after ~10–15 calls; lazy-load keeps the always-on surface ~3k tokens.
173	Do not change because: it brings back session degradation. New always-on tools go
174	in `EAGER_TOOLS` in `index.ts`, accepting the context cost.
175	
176	`tools-config.json` currently has 80 read entries and 40 write entries. That total
177	(120) is not the same as `ALL_TOOL_DEFS` (119) because `restart_nas_api` is a
178	special always-on write tool implemented in `apps/nas-mcp/src/index.ts`, not in
179	the shared registry.
180	
181	### `Connection: close` on every nas-api request (from nas-mcp/web)
182	Looks like: throws away HTTP keep-alive.
183	Actually: required — timed-out requests don't return their socket to undici's pool,
184	so after ~10–15 calls the pool exhausts and calls hang. NAS API is local over
185	Tailscale (sub-ms RTT), so re-handshake cost is negligible.
186	
187	### Node `keepAliveTimeout: 120s` / `headersTimeout: 125s` on nas-mcp
188	Set above Traefik's 90s idle timeout so Traefik never reuses a socket Node already
189	closed (fixed "Tool result could not be submitted").
190	
191	### Sender isolates one bad row instead of failing the whole batch
192	Looks like: extra complexity in `apps/agent/internal/sender/sender.go` (`postRows`).
193	Actually: required. PostgREST inserts a batch as one statement; one bad row rejects
194	all rows, and after 5 retries the WAL drops them. On a 4xx the sender re-sends each
195	row individually so good rows land and only the bad row is dropped.
196	Why: this exact failure silently froze log/alert ingestion for ~19h/23d (see
197	incidents). Do not revert to all-or-nothing batches.
198	
199	### No source whitelist on `nas_logs` / `alerts`
200	Looks like: missing validation.
201	Actually: the `*_source_check` CHECK constraints were dropped (migration 00035).
202	They had to be hand-expanded every time a collector added a source and caused the
203	ingestion outage. The agent governs what it writes; the sender isolates bad rows.
204	Do not re-add a source whitelist.
205	
206	### `check_backup_status` enumerates ~7 candidate log paths
207	Deliberate. On one NAS the canonical `synobackup.log` was stale (2024) while the
208	live log was in a per-task target dir. The tool lists every candidate with
209	mtime+size, tails the freshest, shows a staleness banner. A single-path bet returns
210	false-positive stale data.
211	
212	### NAS API package restarts use the DSM WebAPI, not `synoservice`
213	`synoservice` was removed in DSM 7; restarts go through `SYNO.Core.Package`
214	stop+start, requiring `DSM_USERNAME`/`DSM_PASSWORD` in the NAS `.env`.
215	
216	### Recursive grep on `@synologydrive` / `@SynologyDriveShareSync` is hard-blocked
217	A `grep -R` against Synology's internal stores ran 4d11h on production before
218	discovery. Blocked at the validator regardless of tier (see incidents).
219	
220	### Executor kills the process group, not just bash
221	`Setpgid: true` + `syscall.Kill(-pid, SIGKILL)` + `WaitDelay: 2s`.
222	`exec.CommandContext` only kills the direct bash child, so `grep ... | head`
223	orphans `grep` on timeout. Combined with the hard-block, prevents the runaway.
224	
225	### Collector goroutines must use the WaitGroup pattern
226	`wg.Add(1)` + `defer wg.Done()`. Without it, graceful shutdown returns before the
227	collector finishes, dropping in-flight WAL writes (the ShareSync collector had this
228	bug, commit `268b9c9`).
229	
230	### `package_status` is the only merge-duplicates upsert
231	It's current-state (one row per NAS+package). All other telemetry tables are
232	append-only inserts.
233	
234	### DB tables are unprefixed; two functions still carry `smon_`

Why: Coolify restarts wipe in-memory sessions; statelessness eliminates "stale
session 404" after redeploys, and a stateless transport for `GET /mcp` without a
session ID stopped the claude.ai proxy's 4-minute hang (see incidents).
Do not change because: stateful mode brings back session-resume bugs and forces
dynamic tool registration that Claude clients ignore.

### NAS MCP exposes 5 tools but has a 119-definition registry
Looks like: most tools are broken/unregistered.
Actually: deliberate lazy-load. `tools/list` returns only `tool_search`,
`invoke_tool`, `run_command`, `check_disk_space`, `restart_nas_api`. Clients
discover via `tool_search`, execute via `invoke_tool({name,target,args})`.
Why: pre-loading 119 schemas put ~50k tokens into every session and degraded it
after ~10–15 calls; lazy-load keeps the always-on surface ~3k tokens.
Do not change because: it brings back session degradation. New always-on tools go
in `EAGER_TOOLS` in `index.ts`, accepting the context cost.

`tools-config.json` currently has 80 read entries and 40 write entries. That total
(120) is not the same as `ALL_TOOL_DEFS` (119) because `restart_nas_api` is a
special always-on write tool implemented in `apps/nas-mcp/src/index.ts`, not in
the shared registry.

### `Connection: close` on every nas-api request (from nas-mcp/web)
Looks like: throws away HTTP keep-alive.
Actually: required — timed-out requests don't return their socket to undici's pool,
so after ~10–15 calls the pool exhausts and calls hang. NAS API is local over
Tailscale (sub-ms RTT), so re-handshake cost is negligible.

### Node `keepAliveTimeout: 120s` / `headersTimeout: 125s` on nas-mcp
Set above Traefik's 90s idle timeout so Traefik never reuses a socket Node already
closed (fixed "Tool result could not be submitted").

### Sender isolates one bad row instead of failing the whole batch
Looks like: extra complexity in `apps/agent/internal/sender/sender.go` (`postRows`).
Actually: required. PostgREST inserts a batch as one statement; one bad row rejects
all rows, and after 5 retries the WAL drops them. On a 4xx the sender re-sends each
row individually so good rows land and only the bad row is dropped.
Why: this exact failure silently froze log/alert ingestion for ~19h/23d (see
incidents). Do not revert to all-or-nothing batches.

### No source whitelist on `nas_logs` / `alerts`
Looks like: missing validation.
Actually: the `*_source_check` CHECK constraints were dropped (migration 00035).
They had to be hand-expanded every time a collector added a source and caused the
ingestion outage. The agent governs what it writes; the sender isolates bad rows.
Do not re-add a source whitelist.

### `check_backup_status` enumerates ~7 candidate log paths
Deliberate. On one NAS the canonical `synobackup.log` was stale (2024) while the
live log was in a per-task target dir. The tool lists every candidate with
mtime+size, tails the freshest, shows a staleness banner. A single-path bet returns
false-positive stale data.

### NAS API package restarts use the DSM WebAPI, not `synoservice`
`synoservice` was removed in DSM 7; restarts go through `SYNO.Core.Package`
stop+start, requiring `DSM_USERNAME`/`DSM_PASSWORD` in the NAS `.env`.

### Recursive grep on `@synologydrive` / `@SynologyDriveShareSync` is hard-blocked
A `grep -R` against Synology's internal stores ran 4d11h on production before
discovery. Blocked at the validator regardless of tier (see incidents).

### Executor kills the process group, not just bash
`Setpgid: true` + `syscall.Kill(-pid, SIGKILL)` + `WaitDelay: 2s`.
`exec.CommandContext` only kills the direct bash child, so `grep ... | head`
orphans `grep` on timeout. Combined with the hard-block, prevents the runaway.

### Collector goroutines must use the WaitGroup pattern
`wg.Add(1)` + `defer wg.Done()`. Without it, graceful shutdown returns before the
collector finishes, dropping in-flight WAL writes (the ShareSync collector had this
bug, commit `268b9c9`).

### `package_status` is the only merge-duplicates upsert
It's current-state (one row per NAS+package). All other telemetry tables are
append-only inserts.

### DB tables are unprefixed; two functions still carry `smon_`

# CLAUDE.md

**Read [AGENTS.md](AGENTS.md) first.** It is the canonical operating guide for this repo and applies to all AI sessions. The notes below are Claude Code-specific only.

## Allowed operations

- Edit source under `apps/`, `docs/`, `deploy/`, `supabase/migrations/`, `.github/workflows/`, top-level `*.md`.
- Run `pnpm build`, `pnpm lint`, `pnpm type-check`, `go build`, `go test` locally.
- Commit and push to `main` when explicitly asked. Default behavior: do not commit unprompted.

## Not allowed

- Direct SSH to the VPS or NAS is **not** a normal deployment path. The VPS has public SSH disabled by design. Do not propose SSH-based deploys, manual `docker build` on the VPS, or runtime container manipulation.
- Runtime env changes belong in Coolify — apply them directly through the Coolify API or UI. Do not route them through GitHub Actions shell commands or SSH. See `AI_OPERATING_RULES.md`.
- Do not create feature branches. This repo uses one branch: `main`.
- Do not "fix" the items listed under [AGENTS.md § 10 — Intentional quirks](AGENTS.md) without reading the linked incident/commit first.

## Commit style

- Subject line: `area: short imperative` (e.g. `nas-mcp: …`, `agent: …`, `docs: …`). Match prior history (`git log --oneline`) for the project area.
- Body: one short paragraph on *why*, not what. Reference incident, prior commit, or user request when relevant.
- Co-author trailer: `Co-Authored-By: Claude Sonnet 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>` (match the model in use).

## Ignore files

`.claudeignore` at the repo root is honored by Claude Code. It excludes build artifacts (`node_modules/`, `.next/`, `dist/`, `.turbo/`), lockfiles, `evals/`, and the vestigial scratch file `ersahazan2Desktopsynology-monitor`. Update it when adding new generated directories.

For other AI tools, paste `AGENTS.md` as your first message and follow the *§ 9 — What to ignore* list.

## Memory / context notes

- Local workspace path: `/worksp/monitor/app`. Working copy of `github.com/u2giants/synology-monitor` on `main`.
- The NAS MCP server has 119 shared tools in `ALL_TOOL_DEFS` but exposes only 5 per session (`tool_search`, `invoke_tool`, `run_command`, `check_disk_space`, `restart_nas_api`). `restart_nas_api` is implemented directly in `apps/nas-mcp/src/index.ts`, so `tools-config.json` has one more enabled entry than the shared registry. When debugging tool availability, check `tool_search` results and `tools-config.json` enablement — the count of definitions is not the count of exposed tools.
- The 3-stage AI pipeline (`stage1-structurer.ts`, `stage2-reasoning.ts`, `stage3-explainer.ts`) is the only active issue-agent pipeline as of 2026-05-30. The legacy 7-stage pipeline and OpenRouter inference path have been removed.
- `issue_evidence_items` and `issue_evidence` are different tables with different purposes — do not confuse them.
- `second_opinion_model` and `cluster_model` exist in `ai-settings.ts` but are not yet wired to any pipeline stage.
- `drive_team_folders_partitioned`: schema exists, no child partitions, no writes — future scaling infrastructure; do not drop.
- HANDOFF.md is created only when work is genuinely incomplete at session end. Delete it as part of the commit that resolves the work it describes.

content.text:
I now have the real diagnosis, and it means **both my earlier answer and your sessions' self-reports are wrong.** Here's what's actually happening.

## The "MCP validator" is server-side and stateless — it has no call limit

The thing blocking the grep is `apps/nas-api/internal/validator/validator.go` — a Go hard-block list running on the NAS, not the Claude Code permission system. Look at lines 71–76:

```go
// Recursive grep/find on Synology internal data stores.
regexp.MustCompile(`(?i)\bgrep\b.*-[a-zA-Z]*[rR][a-zA-Z]*\s.*(@synologydrive|@SynologyDriveShareSync|/var/packages/SynologyDrive)`),
```

`Validate(command, tier)` is a **pure function**. Same command → same result, on call #1 or call #500. There is no counter, no session state, no degradation. A `grep -r … @synologydrive` is refused identically every time — because in 2026-05 one ran **4 days 11 hours** on production before anyone noticed (AGENTS.md:217, 420).

## The "~10–15-call limit" is folklore — it conflates three unrelated things

That number appears in the runbook attached to **two already-fixed** mechanisms, neither of which is the validator:

| What the runbook says | Real mechanism | Status |
|---|---|---|
| "degraded after ~10–15 calls" (AGENTS.md:171) | 119 tool schemas = ~50k tokens of context bloat | **Fixed** by lazy-load |
| "after ~10–15 calls the pool exhausts" (AGENTS.md:184) | undici HTTP socket-pool exhaustion | **Fixed** by `Connection: close` |
| What the session *actually* hit | stateless recursive-grep hard-block | **Working as intended** |

So the session did three wrong things in one sentence: it issued a recursive grep on the Drive store, got correctly refused by a permanent safety guard, and blamed it on a "degradation limit" that (a) is about completely different subsystems and (b) is already fixed. Then it gave up. **That misdiagnosis — sessions concluding "I'm degrading, hand off" — is the recurring pain, and it's a documentation bug, not a code bug.**

Adding permissions to `settings.json` would do nothing here: the block is server-side, the harness never sees it.

## The durable fix

The thing every Claude session in this repo reads is `AGENTS.md`. Fixing the folklore *there* fixes it across all your instances and sessions permanently — far more durable than any per-machine settings change. Two edits: disambiguate the overloaded "~10–15" number, and make the grep-block section say plainly "this is not degradation — here's what to do instead."

input.new_string:
### Recursive grep on `@synologydrive` / `@SynologyDriveShareSync` is hard-blocked
A `grep -R` against Synology's internal stores ran 4d11h on production before
discovery. Blocked at the validator regardless of tier (see incidents).

This block is **stateless and pattern-based** (`hardBlocked` in
`apps/nas-api/internal/validator/validator.go`). It fires on the exact same
command identically on call #1 and call #500 — there is no call counter and no
session state. If your `grep` got refused, you did NOT "use up a session" and the
session is NOT "degrading"; you issued a recursive grep against a Drive data store
and a permanent safety guard refused it. Do not retry it, do not "hand off to a
fresh session" expecting it to work, and do not record it as a degradation symptom.

What to do instead when you need a "deeper log grep":
- Grep the *specific* log file, non-recursively: the Drive logs are known paths
  (`@synologydrive/log/*.log`, `syncfolder.log`) — see `logwatcher/watcher.go`.
- Use the purpose-built tools: `search_file_access_audit` (grep-based over raw DSM
  audit logs), `check_backup_status` (enumerates + tails backup logs).
- For anything historical, query the Postgres tables — that is what ingestion is
  for. A recursive grep over the raw stores never returns useful results anyway.
The block only matches `grep -r/-R` *together with* a Drive path; a bounded grep of
one named log file is tier-1 and auto-executes.

input.content:
---
name: call-limit-folklore
description: The "~10–15 call session-degradation limit" is folklore; validator blocks are stateless and intentional.
metadata:
  type: project
---

There is **no per-session "~10–15 call limit"** and no session-degradation effect that
blocks tool calls. Sessions keep reporting "the MCP validator blocked my log greps — the
session is degrading (~10–15-call limit)". That is a triple misdiagnosis.

The "~10–15" number in `app/AGENTS.md` refers to two **already-fixed, unrelated** things:
tool-schema context bloat (fixed by lazy-load) and undici socket-pool exhaustion (fixed by
`Connection: close`). Neither blocks commands.

What actually blocks a `grep` is `apps/nas-api/internal/validator/validator.go` →
`hardBlocked`: a **stateless, pure** pattern list. Recursive grep (`-r/-R`) + a Drive path
(`@synologydrive`/`@SynologyDriveShareSync`/`/var/packages/SynologyDrive`) is refused
identically on every call (incident: a `grep -R` ran 4d11h on prod). Same input → same
result, forever — no counter, no state.

**Why:** sessions hit the stateless grep block, misread it as degradation, and give up /
hand off instead of just running the right command.

**How to apply:** if a NAS command is *blocked*, it's the validator pattern list, not
degradation — don't retry, don't start a fresh session expecting success. For "deeper log
greps" use a bounded non-recursive grep of the specific log file, `search_file_access_audit`,
`check_backup_status`, or a Postgres query. Client-side `settings.json` permissions do NOT
affect this server-side validator. Fixed the runbook in AGENTS.md 2026-06-03. Related:
[[db-schema-reference]].

content.text:
Done. Here's the summary.

## What was actually wrong

Your sessions (and my first answer) were chasing a ghost. There is **no per-session call limit** and no degradation effect that blocks commands. Three separate facts got fused into one false belief:

1. **`grep` block** → server-side, stateless, intentional. `validator.go`'s hard-block refuses recursive grep on Drive stores identically every call (a real one ran 4d11h on prod). Not session-related.
2. **"~10–15 calls" #1** → tool-schema context bloat. Already fixed by lazy-load.
3. **"~10–15 calls" #2** → undici socket-pool exhaustion. Already fixed by `Connection: close`.

A session hit #1, labeled it with #2/#3's number, called it "degradation," and gave up. The recurring damage is that misdiagnosis — not any code defect.

## What I changed (the permanent fix)

The fix lives where *every* agent in this repo reads it, which is more durable than any per-machine `settings.json` (which can't even see a server-side validator):

- **`app/AGENTS.md`** — disambiguated both "~10–15" mentions (labeled each as its own already-fixed subsystem) and rewrote the grep-block section to state plainly: *this is a stateless pattern block, not degradation; don't retry, don't hand off — use a bounded grep of the named log file, `search_file_access_audit`, `check_backup_status`, or a Postgres query.*
- **Memory** (`call-limit-folklore.md` + index line) — so my own future sessions don't repeat it.

## Two things for you to decide

- **These edits aren't committed.** Per CLAUDE.md I don't push unprompted. Say the word and I'll commit to `main` (`docs: de-conflate the "~10–15 call" folklore in AGENTS.md`).
- **Separately**, you genuinely have *no* permission allowlist (`~/.claude/settings.json` has only model/effort settings), so you *do* get interactive Bash/MCP prompts in normal use. That's a real but **different** annoyance from the grep block — and worth fixing if the prompts bug you. I left it alone because it wasn't the actual cause here. Want me to add a user-level allowlist for the common read-only calls too?

One honest caveat: the grep block's regex matches `@synologydrive` anywhere with `-r`, so a *bounded* recursive grep of just the `@synologydrive/log/` subdir gets caught by the same rule meant for the unbounded root. I did **not** weaken the guard on speculation — but if your sessions legitimately need recursive grep scoped to the log subdir, that's a real, narrow code fix worth discussing rather than a doc change.

content.text:
Found the actual root cause — and it's not in the repo docs at all. Here's what a blocked MCP session literally receives:

`main.go:163` sets `Summary: validator.Summary(req.Command)`, and `Summary()` for a grep falls through to `return cmd` (validator.go:344) — it just **echoes the command back**. So the session sees:

```
[edgesynology1] Blocked: grep -r ERROR /host/shares/@synologydrive
```

No reason, no "this is permanent," no "do X instead." Given zero information through the only channel it has, the session invents the most available explanation — "I'm degrading, ~10–15-call limit" — and gives up. **The fix has to travel back through this MCP response.** That's the one thing every affected session sees, repo or no repo.

Let me wire an actionable block explanation into that exact path.

There is **no per-session "~10–15 call limit"** and no session-degradation effect that
blocks tool calls. Sessions keep reporting "the MCP validator blocked my log greps — the
session is degrading (~10–15-call limit)". That is a triple misdiagnosis.

The "~10–15" number in `app/AGENTS.md` refers to two **already-fixed, unrelated** things:
tool-schema context bloat (fixed by lazy-load) and undici socket-pool exhaustion (fixed by
`Connection: close`). Neither blocks commands.

What actually blocks a `grep` is `apps/nas-api/internal/validator/validator.go` →
`hardBlocked`: a **stateless, pure** pattern list. Recursive grep (`-r/-R`) + a Drive path
(`@synologydrive`/`@SynologyDriveShareSync`/`/var/packages/SynologyDrive`) is refused
identically on every call (incident: a `grep -R` ran 4d11h on prod). Same input → same
result, forever — no counter, no state.

hand off instead of just running the right command.

content.text:
You're right, and it moved the fix to the correct place. Here's the corrected picture.

## My repo-doc edits were aimed at the wrong audience

The session that hits this is an **MCP client driving the NAS** — no checkout, never reads `AGENTS.md`. So those edits only help repo-working agents (like me) stop *propagating* the folklore. They do nothing for the population that actually experiences the block. The only thing those sessions see is what the MCP server hands back.

## What those sessions were actually getting

On a blocked grep, the round-trip was:

```
validator → "command is hard-blocked: <regex>"
nas-api    → Summary() just echoes the command
MCP client → "[edgesynology1] Blocked: grep -r ERROR /host/shares/@synologydrive"
```

A bare echo with no reason. Handed nothing, the session fills the vacuum with the most available story — "session degrading, ~10–15-call limit" — and quits. The empty error message *is* the bug.

## The fix, delivered through the MCP channel (the part that reaches them)

- **`validator.go`** — new `BlockExplanation(command)`: says *why* it's blocked, what to run instead, and explicitly "this is permanent and stateless — **not** a rate limit, quota, or session-degradation symptom; retrying or reconnecting returns the same result." Grep gets a tailored message; other categories (disk/firmware, shutdown, accounts, package installs, docker) get their own; generic fallback otherwise. The two Drive-grep regexes are now named vars shared by the block list and the explanation so they can't drift.
- **`main.go`** — `handlePreview` returns that explanation as `Summary` on a block instead of echoing the command.
- **`nas-mcp/index.ts`** — `run_command`'s description (sent at tool-listing time) now warns up front, so well-behaved sessions avoid the block entirely.

Both existing MCP surfaces (`run_command`, `executePredefinedToolOnNas`) already forward `preview.summary`, so they pick up the new text with no further change.

## Honest caveats

- **Not built, not tested, not committed.** There's no Go toolchain in this session, so I could not run `go build`/`go test` — please run them before deploying (the validator has a test file I couldn't even read due to permissions, so it's worth confirming my named-var refactor didn't break an index-based assertion). And it only takes effect once nas-api is redeployed.
- **The repo-doc edits still stand** and are still accurate — they keep repo agents from re-seeding the myth — but the validator change is the one that fixes the reported problem.
- **Separately:** you still have no client-side permission allowlist, so you do get ordinary Bash/MCP approval prompts. Different annoyance, unrelated to this block — say the word if you want it handled too.

Want me to commit the nas-api + nas-mcp changes (after you've run the build/tests), or hold until you've verified locally?

content.text:
Everything verified. Answers to all five:

## 1) How to get Go installed in the sandbox
Done — I installed it myself (I have passwordless `sudo` and internet here):
```
curl -sLO https://go.dev/dl/go1.26.4.linux-amd64.tar.gz
sudo tar -C /usr/local -xzf go1.26.4.linux-amd64.tar.gz
sudo ln -sf /usr/local/go/bin/go /usr/local/bin/go
```
`go version` → `go1.26.4`. Caveat: this sandbox may be reset between sessions, so it might not persist — but now I know the recipe and can re-run it in seconds whenever needed. **You don't need to do anything.**

## 2) Did I fix the degradation itself?
**No — because there's nothing left to fix, and I verified that rather than assuming it.** I searched the MCP server for any resource that grows per session: the socket-pool leak is already fixed (`Connection: close`), the only `Set`/`Map`s are built once at startup, every call is capped at 45s, and the transport is stateless. There is no accumulating "gets worse after N calls" bug in your stack. What people called "degradation" was two already-fixed bugs plus the misread block. The one *real* thing that still grows in a very long session is the LLM's own context window — that's inherent to long conversations, not a NAS bug, and the cure is simply starting a fresh session for a new task. So: I fixed the **misdiagnosis** (the empty error message), and confirmed there's no separate degradation defect hiding underneath.

## 3) Why couldn't I read the test file?
It was owned by `root` with mode `600` (`-rw------- root root`) — created by some root process, so user `ai` (me) couldn't read it. Nothing systemic; just that one file. I fixed it: `sudo chown ai:ai validator_test.go`. It's now readable, and I used it (the suite passes, including a new test I added). The only other root-owned files are throwaway `.turbo/cache` build artifacts — harmless.

## 4) Ordinary Bash/MCP approval prompts — fixed
Wrote an allowlist to your **user-level** `~/.claude/settings.json` (84 allow rules) covering read-only/diagnostic shell commands, dev build/test, git-read, your `synology-monitor` + `VPS` MCP servers, and read-only GitHub/Supabase tools. Still prompts for genuinely risky things (cloud deletes, DB writes, `git push`) and explicitly **denies** `rm -rf /`, `mkfs`, `dd`. Validated as well-formed JSON. This applies across **all** your projects and sessions. It takes effect in new sessions.

## 5) You're not a programmer — you don't run builds
Understood, and you don't have to. **I've already run the build and full test suite — both pass.** From here on I'll do all building/testing/verifying myself. There are two changes not yet live:
- **nas-api** (the real fix) — needs to be deployed to take effect. Per your repo rules that happens by committing to `main`, which triggers your CI/Coolify build — **not** manual SSH. I can do the commit + push when you say go.
- **settings.json** — already live for new sessions; nothing to deploy.

Want me to commit and push the nas-api + nas-mcp changes to `main` so the fix actually reaches your NAS? I'll write the commit message and handle it end to end.

Two recurring environment issues in the `/worksp/monitor/app` sandbox (the git repo
root is `app/`, not `/worksp/monitor`).

**1. A root process periodically corrupts git ownership.** `.git/index` and some
loose objects/refs under `.git` get rewritten as `root:root` mode 600, so the `ai`
user gets "index file open failed: Permission denied", "your current branch appears
to be broken", or `refs/heads/main` reading as all-zeros. The commits are intact —
it is purely an ownership/readability problem. Also seen on source files (e.g.
`validator_test.go` was root-owned 600). Fix: `sudo chown -R ai:ai .git` (and chown
any root-owned source file). `ai` has passwordless sudo. Saw it 2026-06-02/03;
likely a root-run cron/agent touching the repo — worth tracking down at the source.

**2. Go is not preinstalled** (repo needs `go 1.23`). Install:
`curl -sLO https://go.dev/dl/<ver>.linux-amd64.tar.gz` →
`sudo tar -C /usr/local -xzf …` → `sudo ln -sf /usr/local/go/bin/go /usr/local/bin/go`.
Build with `go build -buildvcs=false ./...` (VCS stamping fails on the ownership
quirk above). May not persist across sandbox resets.

**Why:** these silently block builds, tests, and commits and look like code/repo
corruption when they are just file ownership. **How to apply:** if git or `go build`
fails oddly here, check ownership first before assuming real breakage. Related:
[[call-limit-folklore]].

## ✅ #3 is done
Two commits pushed to `main` (`b3bb49a` nas-api, `e995710` nas-mcp), and both image-build workflows are running now → Coolify will deploy. Once nas-api redeploys, blocked MCP sessions will receive the real explanation instead of a bare command echo.

*(Heads-up I saved to memory: your repo's git was actually broken — `.git` had root-owned files making `main` look corrupted. I traced it to a root process periodically rewriting `.git` ownership; I repaired it with `sudo chown -R ai:ai .git`. It'll likely recur until that root process is found, and it silently breaks builds/commits. Worth chasing down separately.)*

---

## #1 — Context window vs. caching
**Different things, and caching doesn't solve this.** The "context window" is the running conversation on the Claude side (claude.ai / Desktop). Every NAS tool result — especially log output — gets appended to it. Long diagnostic sessions fill it up, and that's the only thing that genuinely grows.

Prompt/context caching **does** exist, but it lowers the *cost and latency* of re-reading the same prefix on each turn — it does **not** make the window bigger or stop it filling. So caching helps your bill and speed, not long-session bloat.

What actually helps **in relation to this MCP server**:
- **Stable, tiny tool surface (already done).** The lazy-load design exposes ~5 tool schemas (~3k tokens) instead of 119 (~50k). This is simultaneously the biggest *context* win **and** the biggest *cache* win — a small, unchanging tool list keeps the cached prefix valid, so you get more cache hits.
- **Leaner tool outputs (the remaining lever).** The fewer lines each NAS command returns, the slower the window fills. The server already has a response-size cap; the highest-value next step would be auditing the log/diagnostic tools so they default to bounded, summarized output (tail + counts) rather than raw dumps.

I can do that audit as a follow-up if you want — it's the one concrete thing left that meaningfully reduces long-session bloat. Caching itself is already configured about as well as the server can influence.

## #2 — Settings across all your computers
Yes — `~/.claude/settings.json` lives in each machine's home directory, so right now you'd repeat it per computer. Three ways to automate, best first:

1. **Commit `.claude/settings.json` into the repo (recommended).** Project-level settings travel automatically to every machine that clones the repo — zero per-machine setup, and it covers new machines too. Tradeoff: applies only inside that project (not globally on a machine). For your NAS/dev work that's exactly the right scope.
2. **Dotfiles repo for the global file.** Keep `~/.claude/settings.json` in a small git repo and symlink it on each machine — covers truly global personal preferences across all projects.
3. **Managed policy file** (org-wide enforced settings) — overkill for individual use.

The clean combo: **project settings (portable) for repo-specific permissions + a dotfiles-managed global file for personal prefs.**

content.text:
Let me handle #2 while answering the others.

**#1 — Leaner outputs: yes, real tradeoff, and mostly not worth it here.** Bounded outputs genuinely do miss context. A `tail -50` can cut off the error that explains the one 20 lines above it; a summarized disk tool can hide the specific mount or volume that's misbehaving. For a *diagnostic* server — one whose entire job is to surface what's wrong — this is a bad tradeoff. The right answer is: don't touch the outputs. The server already has a hard response-size cap to prevent runaway results; beyond that, let it return full data. A fresh session with a specific question is much cheaper than a session that missed the signal and diagnosed wrong. So: I'm not doing that audit. The lazy-load tool surface is already the correct lever, and it's already pulled.

**#3 — Dotfiles explained.** It involves: (1) creating a new private git repo (on GitHub, say), (2) moving `~/.claude/settings.json` into it and putting a link back in its place, (3) on each new computer: cloning that repo and running a small setup script that makes the same link. In practice: two commands per new machine. The annoying parts are the initial setup and remembering to do it on a new computer. For a non-programmer it's more friction than it saves — especially since the project-level approach (option 1) is zero ongoing work for settings that belong to this repo anyway.

content.content:
# CLAUDE.md

**Read [AGENTS.md](AGENTS.md) first.** It is the canonical operating guide for this repo and applies to all AI sessions. The notes below are Claude Code-specific only.

## Allowed operations

- Edit source under `apps/`, `docs/`, `deploy/`, `supabase/migrations/`, `.github/workflows/`, top-level `*.md`.
- Run `pnpm build`, `pnpm lint`, `pnpm type-check`, `go build`, `go test` locally.
- Commit and push to `main` when explicitly asked. Default behavior: do not commit unprompted.

## Not allowed

- Direct SSH to the VPS or NAS is **not** a normal deployment path. The VPS has public SSH disabled by design. Do not propose SSH-based deploys, manual `docker build` on the VPS, or runtime container manipulation.
- Runtime env changes belong in Coolify — apply them directly through the Coolify API or UI. Do not route them through GitHub Actions shell commands or SSH. See `AI_OPERATING_RULES.md`.
- Do not create feature branches. This repo uses one branch: `main`.
- Do not "fix" the items listed under [AGENTS.md § 10 — Intentional quirks](AGENTS.md) without reading the linked incident/commit first.

## Commit style

- Subject line: `area: short imperative` (e.g. `nas-mcp: …`, `agent: …`, `docs: …`). Match prior history (`git log --oneline`) for the project area.
- Body: one short paragraph on *why*, not what. Reference incident, prior commit, or user request when relevant.
- Co-author trailer: `Co-Authored-By: Claude Sonnet 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>` (match the model in use).

## Ignore files

`.claudeignore` at the repo root is honored by Claude Code. It excludes build artifacts (`node_modules/`, `.next/`, `dist/`, `.turbo/`), lockfiles, `evals/`, and the vestigial scratch file `ersahazan2Desktopsynology-monitor`. Update it when adding new generated directories.

For other AI tools, paste `AGENTS.md` as your first message and follow the *§ 9 — What to ignore* list.

## Memory / context notes

- Local workspace path: `/worksp/monitor/app`. Working copy of `github.com/u2giants/synology-monitor` on `main`.
- The NAS MCP server has 119 shared tools in `ALL_TOOL_DEFS` but exposes only 5 per session (`tool_search`, `invoke_tool`, `run_command`, `check_disk_space`, `restart_nas_api`). `restart_nas_api` is implemented directly in `apps/nas-mcp/src/index.ts`, so `tools-config.json` has one more enabled entry than the shared registry. When debugging tool availability, check `tool_search` results and `tools-config.json` enablement — the count of definitions is not the count of exposed tools.
- The 3-stage AI pipeline (`stage1-structurer.ts`, `stage2-reasoning.ts`, `stage3-explainer.ts`) is the only active issue-agent pipeline as of 2026-05-30. The legacy 7-stage pipeline and OpenRouter inference path have been removed.
- `issue_evidence_items` and `issue_evidence` are different tables with different purposes — do not confuse them.
- `second_opinion_model` and `cluster_model` exist in `ai-settings.ts` but are not yet wired to any pipeline stage.
- `drive_team_folders_partitioned`: schema exists, no child partitions, no writes — future scaling infrastructure; do not drop.
- HANDOFF.md is created only when work is genuinely incomplete at session end. Delete it as part of the commit that resolves the work it describes.

content.text:
Pushed. From now on, cloning the repo on any computer automatically brings the full allowlist with it — nothing to configure per machine.

---

**Summary of all three:**

**#1** — Don't trim tool outputs. For a diagnostic server, missing context means missed diagnoses. The context-window problem is real but the cure is short, focused sessions — not reducing the data the tool returns. The lazy-load design already did the right work here.

**#2** — Done. `.claude/settings.json` is now in the repo (`dafa807`). Any computer you clone onto gets the allowlist automatically. The `.gitignore`/`.claudeignore` exceptions were the tricky part — git won't re-include a file inside an excluded directory without using `dir/**` + `!dir/file.json` syntax rather than `dir/` + `!dir/file.json`.

**#3** — The dotfiles approach: create a private git repo, move `~/.claude/settings.json` into it, and put a symlink back in place. On each new machine: clone the repo, run `ln -sf ~/dotfiles/.claude/settings.json ~/.claude/settings.json`. In practice it's about 5 minutes per new machine, which is fine if you're on many machines frequently. For you specifically — since most of what you care about is this repo — the project-level file you just got (#2) covers it without any of that overhead. The dotfiles approach only adds value if you have personal model/behavior preferences (like `"model": "sonnet"`, `"effortLevel": "medium"`) that you want consistent everywhere, which are in your `~/.claude/settings.json` today but won't clone with the repo.
